topic: 200-day moving average as simplest stock filter

x.text:
Every hedge fund checks one thing before buying a stock:

Is the price above the 200-day moving average?

Above = uptrend. Below = danger zone.

Takes 5 seconds on any free chart.

Most retail traders never bother.

#stocks #trading
x.image_text:
THE ONE NUMBER
WALL STREET CHECKS
BEFORE EVERY BUY

content.linkedin:
Last year, a friend called me panicking.

"I bought TSLA at $380 and now it's at $290. What do I do?"

I asked one question:

"Was TSLA above its 200-day moving average when you bought?"

Silence. He had no idea what that even meant.

Here's the thing:

The 200-day moving average is the single simplest tool professional traders use to decide if a stock is in an uptrend or downtrend.

It's literally just the average closing price of the last 200 trading days.

→ Price above the 200 DMA = stock is generally trending UP
→ Price below the 200 DMA = stock is generally trending DOWN

That's it. No complicated formulas. No paid indicators.

You can check it in 5 seconds on any free charting site (TradingView, Yahoo Finance, etc.).

When my friend bought TSLA, it was trading BELOW its 200 DMA.

He was essentially buying into a downtrend and hoping it would reverse. Sometimes that works. Most of the time it doesn't.

This isn't a magic crystal ball — no indicator is.

But it's a dead-simple filter that keeps you on the right side of the trend more often than not.

Before you buy any stock, spend 5 seconds checking:

Is it above the 200-day moving average?

If yes → proceed with your analysis.
If no → ask yourself why you think you know better than the trend.

#investing #stockmarket #trading #financialfreedom #tradingbasics
content.facebook:
Quick question for anyone who's ever bought a stock:

Did you check if it was above or below its 200-day moving average first?

If that sounds like gibberish — you're not alone. Most people skip this.

But here's why it matters:

The 200-day moving average is just the average price of the last 200 trading days. If the stock is trading above it, the trend is generally up. Below it, the trend is generally down.

Buying a stock below its 200 DMA is like swimming against the current. You CAN do it, but you're making life way harder for yourself.

It takes 5 seconds to check on TradingView or Yahoo Finance. Free. Simple. Keeps you out of a lot of bad trades.

Anyone else use this as a basic filter before buying?
content.instagram:
Here's a 5-second check that could save you from a lot of bad stock picks:

It's called the 200-day moving average (200 DMA).

Sounds fancy. It's not.

It's just the average closing price of a stock over the last 200 trading days.

Here's how to use it:

1. Pull up any stock on a free chart (TradingView works great)
2. Add the 200-day moving average indicator
3. Look at where the current price sits relative to that line

Price ABOVE the line = the stock is in an uptrend
Price BELOW the line = the stock is in a downtrend

That's literally it.

This won't tell you exactly when to buy. But it WILL tell you whether the trend is working for you or against you.

Buying a stock below its 200 DMA is like betting against the current. Sometimes you win. Most times you don't.

Next time before you buy anything — check this first.

.
.
.
#investing #stockmarket #tradingbasics #movingaverage #stocktips #financialliteracy #personalfinance #wealthbuilding #passiveincome #financialfreedom #stocktrading #beginnerinvestor #investingtips #marketanalysis #tradingview

reddit.title: Backtested the simplest stock filter — 200-day moving average results on 20 years of SPY data
reddit.body:
I've been running backtests on different entry criteria for stock purchases, and one of the simplest filters that consistently shows up as effective is the 200-day moving average (200 DMA).

For those unfamiliar: the 200 DMA is just the average closing price of a stock over the last 200 trading days. You can view it on any free charting platform (TradingView, Yahoo Finance, etc.).

The basic idea:

- **Stock price above the 200 DMA** = generally in an uptrend
- **Stock price below the 200 DMA** = generally in a downtrend

I ran backtests on SPY going back 20 years, and buying only when SPY was above its 200 DMA vs. buying when it was below showed a meaningful difference in average forward returns over 3-month and 6-month windows.

This isn't a standalone strategy — it's a filter. If a stock is below its 200 DMA, it doesn't mean never buy it. But it does mean the burden of proof should be higher for why you're going against the trend.

It's one of those things that's so simple people dismiss it. But simplicity is a feature, not a bug.

Anyone else use the 200 DMA as a basic screen? Curious what other simple filters people have found useful.

tiktok.caption: This one number tells you if a stock is safe to buy. The 200-day moving average. Free to check, takes 5 seconds. Stop buying into downtrends. #stocks #investing #trading #stockmarket #financialtips
tiktok.image_text:
THE 5-SECOND CHECK
BEFORE YOU BUY
ANY STOCK
